pairs.question: Where and when was Henri Étienne Sainte-Claire Deville born?
pairs.answer: Henri Étienne Sainte-Claire Deville was born on 11 March 1818 on the island of St Thomas in the Danish West Indies, where his father served as the French consul.

pairs.question: What were the two academic degrees Henri Étienne Sainte-Claire Deville obtained in 1844?
pairs.answer: In 1844, Deville graduated with both a doctor of medicine and a doctor of science degree.

pairs.question: Which institutions and positions did Deville hold in Paris after returning from Besançon?
pairs.answer: After returning to Paris in 1852, Deville succeeded Antoine Jérôme Balard at the École Normale and later became a professor at the Sorbonne in 1859, replacing J. B. A. Dumas, for whom he had begun lecturing in 1853.

pairs.question: What significant chemical discovery did Deville make in 1849, and why was it notable?
pairs.answer: In 1849, Deville discovered anhydrous nitric acid, also known as nitrogen pentoxide. This was notable as it was the first compound obtained of the so-called 'anhydrides' of monobasic acids, marking an important advancement in chemical science.

pairs.question: Describe the method Deville developed for producing metallic aluminium on a large scale.
pairs.answer: Deville developed a method to prepare metallic aluminium on a large scale using sodium. Additionally, he contributed to the development of sodium production itself, enabling the efficient extraction of aluminium from its compounds.

pairs.question: What notable discovery did Deville make in collaboration with Friedrich Wöhler in 1857?
pairs.answer: Together with Friedrich Wöhler, Deville discovered silicon nitride in 1857, adding another significant compound to the field of chemistry.

pairs.question: How was Deville honored posthumously in Paris?
pairs.answer: In 1885, the rue Sainte-Claire-Deville in the 12th arrondissement of Paris was named in his honor, commemorating his contributions to science.
